introduction.p: Use Figure 2.3.12 in the text (which plots the sine and cosine functions ont the same axes) to assist in answering the following questions.

statement.p: Give an example of the largest interval you can find on which f(t) = \sin(t) is decreasing.

solution.p: The function f(t) = \sin(t) is decreasing on the interval \left[\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{3\pi}{2}\right].

statement.p: Give an example of the largest interval you can find on which f(t) = \sin(t) is decreasing and concave down.

solution.p: The function f(t) = \sin(t) is decreasing and concave down on the interval \left[\frac{\pi}{2}, \pi\right].

statement.p: Give an example of the largest interval you can find on which g(t) = \cos(t) is increasing.

solution.p: The function g(t) = \cos(t) is increasing on the interval [\pi, 2\pi].

statement.p: Give an example of the largest interval you can find on which g(t) = \cos(t) is increasing and concave up.

solution.p: The function g(t) = \cos(t) is increasing and concave up on the interval \left[\pi, \frac{3\pi}{2}\right].

statement.p: Without doing any computation, on which interval is the average rate of change of g(t) = \cos(t) greater: [\pi, \pi+0.1] or [\frac{3\pi}{2}, \frac{3\pi}{2} + 0.1]? Why?

solution.p: The average rate of change of g(t) = \cos(t) is greater on the interval \left[\frac{3\pi}{2}, \frac{3\pi}{2} + 0.1\right] because g(t) has a steeper slope near \frac{3\pi}{2} (a zero crossing) than near \pi (a minimum).

statement.p: In general, how would you characterize the locations on the sine and cosine graphs where the functions are increasing or decreasingly most rapidly?

solution.p: The most rapid average rates of change on both graphs occur near the zero crossing points (where the function crosses the midline).

statement.p: Thinking from the perspective of the unit circle, for which quadrants of the x-y plane is \cos(t) negative for an angle t that lies in that quadrant?

solution.p: The function g(t) = \cos(t) is negative on quadrants II and III.
